tartrazine.name: Tartrazine
tartrazine.detail: EU requires "may have adverse effect on activity and attention in children" warning label.
tartrazine.summary: A synthetic yellow dye linked to hyperactivity in children. EU mandates a warning label; India has no such requirement.
tartrazine.fssaiLimit: Permitted up to 100-200 ppm depending on food category

impacts.effect: Linked to hyperactivity, inattention, and behavioral changes in children, particularly those with ADHD
Triggers allergic reactions (urticaria, angioedema) in ~1 in 10,000 people, especially aspirin-sensitive individuals
May worsen asthma symptoms in sensitive individuals

vulnerableGroups: Children under 12 (behavioral effects)
People with ADHD or hyperactivity disorders
Aspirin-sensitive individuals
Asthmatics
tartrazine.whatToDo: Look for natural alternatives like annatto, turmeric, or beetroot color. Avoid in children's products. Check labels for "colour (102)" or "INS 102".

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in 3-year-old and 8/9-year-old children (Southampton Study)
Scientific Opinion on the re-evaluation of Tartrazine (E 102)
EU Regulation 1333/2008 - Food Additives

carmoisine.name: Carmoisine (Azorubine)
carmoisine.healthEffect: Linked to hyperactivity in children. Not approved for food use in the US.
carmoisine.detail: Azo dye widely used in Indian snacks and sauces but not on the FDA approved food color list.
carmoisine.summary: A synthetic red azo dye. Not approved by the US FDA for food use. EU requires hyperactivity warning labels.
carmoisine.fdaStatus: NOT approved for food use in the US

impacts.effect: Linked to hyperactivity and attention deficits in children (one of the "Southampton Six" colors)
Triggers allergic-type reactions in sensitive individuals, especially those with aspirin intolerance
Some azo dyes can be metabolized into aromatic amines, which raise theoretical carcinogenic concerns; not classified by IARC

vulnerableGroups: Children under 12 (behavioral effects)
People with ADHD
Aspirin-sensitive individuals
Asthmatics
carmoisine.whatToDo: Check labels for "INS 122", "E122", "Carmoisine", or "Colour 122". Avoid in products for children. US residents never see this because FDA doesn't approve it for food.

sources.title: Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in children (Southampton Study)
Scientific Opinion on the re-evaluation of Azorubine/Carmoisine (E 122)
FDA Color Additives Approved for Use in Food

ponceau 4r.name: Ponceau 4R
ponceau 4r.healthEffect: Linked to hyperactivity in children. Banned in US.
ponceau 4r.detail: Found in many Indian beverages and confectionery. Banned in the US.

erythrosine.name: Erythrosine
erythrosine.detail: FDA banned erythrosine in January 2025, effective January 2027.

sunset yellow.name: Sunset Yellow
sunset yellow.detail: EU requires warning label. Very common in Indian snacks.
sunset yellow.fdaStatus: Approved as FD&C Yellow 6 under 21 CFR 74.706

allura red.name: Allura Red
allura red.detail: EU requires warning label. Found in candies and soft drinks.

brilliant blue.name: Brilliant Blue
brilliant blue.healthEffect: May cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
brilliant blue.detail: Permitted globally but some countries restrict usage levels.

sodium benzoate.name: Sodium Benzoate
sodium benzoate.healthEffect: Can react with Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) in drinks to form benzene, a known human carcinogen.
sodium benzoate.detail: Common in packaged fruit beverages and juices. Risk increases when combined with ascorbic acid (vitamin C), which can convert it to benzene.
sodium benzoate.summary: A common preservative. SAFE on its own, but can react with Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) to form benzene - a known human carcinogen. Many Indian juices contain both.
sodium benzoate.fdaStatus: GRAS, permitted up to 0.1% in food
sodium benzoate.fssaiLimit: Max 120-200 mg/kg depending on food category

impacts.effect: Can combine with ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) to form BENZENE, classified by IARC as "carcinogenic to humans" (Group 1). Multiple Indian soft drinks have tested positive.
impacts.sourceLabel: FDA Investigation of Benzene in Beverages

impacts.effect: Linked to hyperactivity and ADHD symptoms in children when combined with artificial colors (Southampton Study)
Can trigger allergic-type reactions, urticaria, and asthma exacerbation in sensitive individuals

vulnerableGroups: Children (Southampton Study linked to hyperactivity)
People with aspirin or salicylate sensitivity
Asthmatics
Anyone drinking beverages with both sodium benzoate AND Vitamin C
sodium benzoate.whatToDo: Check labels for "E211", "INS 211", or "Sodium Benzoate". Critically — if you see it alongside "Vitamin C", "Ascorbic Acid", or "Citric Acid" in a drink, avoid. Heat and sunlight accelerate benzene formation.

sources.title: Questions and Answers on the Occurrence of Benzene in Soft Drinks
Food additives and hyperactive behaviour in children
Re-evaluation of benzoic acid and sodium benzoate
IARC Monograph Volume 100F: Benzene

potassium bromate.name: Potassium Bromate
potassium bromate.healthEffect: IARC Group 2B carcinogen. Banned in EU, India (2016), and many countries.
potassium bromate.detail: Officially banned in India since 2016 but enforcement is inconsistent. Still found in some bread products.
potassium bromate.fdaStatus: Permitted in US as a dough conditioner, max 75 ppm of flour (21 CFR 136.110). Banned in California effective January 1, 2027 (AB 418).

tbhq.name: TBHQ (Tert-Butylhydroquinone)
tbhq.healthEffect: Permitted but with strict limits. JECFA noted "limited evidence of immunotoxicity" at high doses.
tbhq.detail: Common in instant noodle seasoning sachets, flavored chips, and fried snack seasonings. FSSAI limit: 200 ppm.
tbhq.summary: A synthetic antioxidant used to prevent fats from going rancid. Safe at permitted levels, but animal studies link it to liver and immune effects at high doses.

impacts.effect: JECFA noted "limited evidence of immunotoxicity" at doses above the ADI. Some animal studies show increased inflammation.
Rodent studies show liver damage and enlargement at doses 40-100x the human ADI
High doses linked to stomach tumors in animal studies (rat forestomach)
Anecdotal reports of nausea, vomiting, ringing in ears at high intake

vulnerableGroups: Children (smaller body weight = higher relative exposure)
Daily consumers of multiple processed snacks
People with existing liver conditions
tbhq.whatToDo: Check for "INS 319", "E319", or "TBHQ" on labels. Reduce daily consumption of fried packaged snacks. FSSAI allows up to 200 ppm, so limit intake to stay well below ADI.

sources.title: JECFA 78th Meeting - TBHQ Safety Evaluation
EFSA Opinion on tertiary-Butylhydroquinone (TBHQ)
Carcinogenicity of butylated hydroxyanisole and TBHQ

bha.name: BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole)
bha.healthEffect: IARC classifies as "possibly carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2B). Listed on California Prop 65.
bha.detail: Restricted in EU and California. Still widely used in Indian cooking oils and snacks.
bha.summary: A synthetic antioxidant classified by IARC as "possibly carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2B). On California's Prop 65 cancer list. Still used in India.
bha.fdaStatus: GRAS with limits (0.02% of fat/oil content)
bha.fssaiLimit: Permitted up to 200 ppm in fats and oils

impacts.effect: IARC classifies as "possibly carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2B) based on forestomach tumors in rodents
Listed on California Prop 65 as a chemical "known to the State to cause cancer"
NIH identified BHA as "reasonably anticipated to be an endocrine disruptor" based on animal studies
Animal studies show liver enlargement and enzyme changes at high doses

vulnerableGroups: Children (high exposure via snacks, lower body weight)
Pregnant women (developmental concerns from animal studies)
People with existing cancer risk factors
Heavy consumers of processed fats and oils
bha.whatToDo: Check labels for "INS 320", "E320", or "BHA". Look for products using "natural antioxidants" or "mixed tocopherols" instead. FDA launched a post-market assessment of BHA in February 2026.

sources.title: IARC Monograph Volume 40: BHA, BHT, and other chemicals
Scientific Opinion on BHA (E 320) reevaluation
California Proposition 65 List of Chemicals
NTP Report on Carcinogens - BHA

bht.name: BHT
bht.healthEffect: Possible endocrine disruptor. Under review by multiple agencies.
bht.detail: Found in cereals, chewing gum, and snack foods.

msg.name: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
msg.healthEffect: FDA/JECFA classify as GRAS (safe). Some individuals report headaches, flushing, and sweating ("MSG symptom complex").
msg.detail: Common in instant noodles, chips, and ready-to-eat meal seasonings. Classified as safe at normal dietary levels by FDA, EFSA, and JECFA.
msg.summary: A widely used flavor enhancer. Officially classified as safe by FDA, EFSA, and JECFA but linked to headache, flushing, and sweating in some sensitive individuals.
msg.fssaiLimit: Permitted as flavour enhancer, no maximum limit specified

impacts.effect: Self-reported headaches, flushing, sweating, and muscle tightness in ~1% of population ("MSG symptom complex"). Double-blind studies show mixed results.
Animal studies show high doses can cause neuronal damage in hypothalamus (not replicated at dietary levels)
EFSA 2017 re-evaluation found "ADI of 30 mg/kg should not be exceeded" due to potential adverse effects at higher doses
Rare cases of asthma exacerbation reported in sensitive individuals

vulnerableGroups: People with "MSG sensitivity" (~1% of population)
Children (lower body weight, EFSA notes exposure can exceed ADI)
Asthmatics (rare bronchospasm reports)
Heavy consumers of processed foods with multiple MSG-containing items
msg.whatToDo: Check labels for "E621", "INS 621", "MSG", "monosodium glutamate", or "flavour enhancer 621". EFSA notes most adults exceed ADI from normal diet. Limit packaged foods with multiple MSG sources.
saferAlternatives: Fresh herbs and spices (garam masala, cumin, coriander)
Home-cooked food with no added flavor enhancers

sources.title: Re-evaluation of glutamic acid (E 620), sodium glutamate (E 621)...
Does monosodium glutamate really cause headache? A systematic review
Analysis of adverse reactions to monosodium glutamate

disodium guanylate.name: Disodium Guanylate
disodium guanylate.healthEffect: Purine content — people with gout or hyperuricaemia should limit intake.
disodium guanylate.detail: Flavour enhancer typically combined with MSG. JECFA ADI 'not specified'. FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.535). Purine metabolism → uric acid, relevant for gout patients.

disodium inosinate.name: Disodium Inosinate
disodium inosinate.healthEffect: Purine content — people with gout should limit intake.
disodium inosinate.detail: Flavour enhancer. JECFA ADI 'not specified'. FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.535). Often combined with MSG or disodium guanylate.

titanium dioxide.name: Titanium Dioxide (E171)
titanium dioxide.healthEffect: BANNED as food additive in EU (2022) due to genotoxicity concerns. Still permitted in India.
titanium dioxide.detail: EFSA concluded it can no longer be considered safe. Banned as a food additive in the EU effective August 2022.
titanium dioxide.summary: A white pigment BANNED as a food additive in the EU (2022) because EFSA could not rule out genotoxicity (DNA damage). Still permitted in Indian food.
titanium dioxide.fdaStatus: Permitted as a food color (max 1% by weight)
titanium dioxide.fssaiLimit: Permitted in confectionery, chewing gum, icing

impacts.effect: Classified by IARC as "possibly carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2B) via inhalation. Nanoparticle form raises additional concerns.
EFSA could not rule out genotoxicity (DNA damage) after ingestion. Nanoparticles may accumulate in the gut.
Animal studies suggest immune system effects and intestinal inflammation at high doses

vulnerableGroups: Children (higher consumption of candies and sweets per body weight)
Heavy consumers of white-coated confectionery
People with gut inflammation or IBD
foundInProducts: White-coated chewing gum (Center Fresh, Orbit, Mentos)
Some white medicines and supplement capsules
titanium dioxide.whatToDo: Check labels for "E171", "INS 171", or "Titanium Dioxide". Avoid white-coated confectionery marketed to children. Many companies reformulated after the EU ban.

sources.title: Safety assessment of titanium dioxide (E 171) as a food additive
IARC Monograph Volume 93: Carbon Black, Titanium Dioxide, and Talc
EU Regulation 2022/63 - Banning E171 as food additive

azodicarbonamide.name: Azodicarbonamide
azodicarbonamide.healthEffect: Respiratory sensitizer. Linked to asthma. Banned in EU and Australia.
azodicarbonamide.detail: Used in bread as a bleaching and maturing agent. Also used in yoga mats and shoe soles.

aspartame.name: Aspartame
aspartame.detail: WHO/JECFA maintained the ADI at 40 mg/kg body weight. Found in diet drinks and sugar-free products.
aspartame.fdaStatus: Approved food additive under 21 CFR 172.804 as a general-purpose sweetener

sucralose.name: Sucralose
sucralose.healthEffect: Emerging concerns about gut microbiome disruption and DNA damage at high temperatures
sucralose.detail: Generally considered safer than aspartame. Found in many "sugar-free" Indian products.

carrageenan.name: Carrageenan
carrageenan.healthEffect: Food-grade carrageenan is permitted with EFSA 2018 temporary ADI 75 mg/kg bw/day; the degraded form poligeenan (a different substance not used in food) was IARC Group 2B in 1983.
carrageenan.detail: Seaweed-derived thickener used in dairy products, non-dairy milks, and processed foods. EFSA 2018 (EFSA Journal 2018;16:5238) set a temporary ADI of 75 mg/kg bw/day pending more data on the low-molecular-weight fraction. JECFA 2015 concluded up to 1000 mg/L in infant formula is not of safety concern (contested by some researchers). The degraded form poligeenan has been classified as IARC Group 2B since 1983 but it is NOT the same substance as food-grade carrageenan.

sodium lauryl sulfate.name: Sodium Lauryl Sulfate (SLS)
sodium lauryl sulfate.healthEffect: Skin and eye irritant. Can strip natural oils from skin.
sodium lauryl sulfate.detail: Found in most Indian shampoos and face washes. Not a carcinogen but causes irritation.

parabens.name: Parabens
parabens.healthEffect: EFSA withdrew propylparaben's ADI in 2004 over juvenile-rat reproductive-toxicity data. Methyl and ethyl parabens remain under group ADI 10 mg/kg bw/day with no regulatory endocrine classification.
parabens.detail: Synthetic preservatives used in food, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals. EFSA 2004 (EFSA Journal 2004;2:83) withdrew propylparaben's ADI based on effects on sperm production in juvenile rats and absence of a clear NOAEL. Methyl and ethyl parabens retained a group ADI of 10 mg/kg bw/day. California AB 418 (2023) will prohibit propylparaben in food effective January 1, 2027. EFSA has NOT classified any paraben as a confirmed endocrine disruptor at dietary exposure. In-vitro estrogenic activity has been observed but is weak relative to natural estrogens.
parabens.fdaStatus: Methylparaben GRAS under 21 CFR 184.1490; Propylparaben GRAS under 21 CFR 184.1670 but will be banned from food in California effective January 1, 2027 (AB 418).
parabens.fssaiLimit: Permitted as Class II preservative (FSS Regulations 2011) for methyl parahydroxybenzoic acid and propyl parahydroxybenzoic acid.
foundInProducts: Packaged food preservatives (jams, sauces, baked goods)
parabens.whatToDo: Look for "INS 214-219", "E214-E219", or names "methylparaben", "propylparaben", "ethylparaben" on food labels. Methyl and ethyl parabens have a full EFSA ADI. Propylparaben's ADI was withdrawn by EFSA in 2004 and will be banned in California food from 2027.
saferAlternatives: Natural preservatives like rosemary extract or tocopherols (INS 306-309)

sources.title: EFSA Opinion on parahydroxybenzoates E 214-219 (2004)
California Food Safety Act AB 418 (2023)
21 CFR 184.1490 Methylparaben (FDA)
21 CFR 184.1670 Propylparaben (FDA)

formaldehyde.name: Formaldehyde
formaldehyde.detail: Often hidden as "formaldehyde releasers" like DMDM Hydantoin. Found in some Lakme and salon products.

hydroquinone.name: Hydroquinone
hydroquinone.healthEffect: Linked to ochronosis (skin darkening), organ toxicity. Possible carcinogen.
hydroquinone.detail: Restricted above 2% in India (classified as drug). Found in unregulated fairness creams.

triclosan.name: Triclosan
triclosan.healthEffect: Endocrine disruptor. Disrupts thyroid function. Promotes antibiotic resistance.
triclosan.detail: Banned in EU consumer products. Still found in some Indian soaps and toothpastes.

phthalates.name: Phthalates
phthalates.healthEffect: Endocrine disruptor. Linked to reproductive issues, early puberty, and reduced testosterone.
phthalates.detail: Banned in EU cosmetics and toys. Found in fragrances, nail polish, hair sprays, plastics, and PVC packaging. Often hidden under "fragrance" or "parfum".

bpa.name: BPA (Bisphenol A)
bpa.healthEffect: Strong endocrine disruptor. Mimics estrogen. Linked to infertility, diabetes, and cancer.
bpa.detail: Banned in EU food contact materials (2024). Found in can linings, plastic water bottles, thermal receipts.

bps.name: BPS (Bisphenol S)
bps.healthEffect: Replacement for BPA but equally concerning. Endocrine disruption, metabolic effects.
bps.detail: Used as "BPA-free" replacement but research shows similar hormone-disrupting effects.

oxybenzone.name: Oxybenzone
oxybenzone.healthEffect: Endocrine disruptor. Hormone interference. Allergic reactions.
oxybenzone.detail: Common sunscreen chemical. Banned in Hawaii and Key West for reef damage. EU restricts to 6% max.

octinoxate.name: Octinoxate
octinoxate.healthEffect: Endocrine disruptor. Estrogenic activity. Absorbed through skin.
octinoxate.detail: UV filter in many Indian sunscreens and fairness creams. Found in Fair & Lovely and similar products.

homosalate.name: Homosalate
homosalate.healthEffect: Possible endocrine disruptor. Accumulates in body faster than it breaks down.
homosalate.detail: EU restricts to 7.34% max. Common sunscreen UV filter.

pfas.name: PFAS (Forever Chemicals)
pfas.healthEffect: Persistent in body and environment. Linked to cancer, immune suppression, thyroid disease.
pfas.detail: Found in non-stick cookware, water-resistant packaging, grease-proof food wrappers. Called "forever chemicals" because they never break down.

propylene glycol.name: Propylene Glycol
propylene glycol.healthEffect: Skin irritant for sensitive individuals. Can enhance absorption of other chemicals.
propylene glycol.detail: Found in cosmetics, processed foods, e-cigarettes. Generally safe at low concentrations.

dioxane.name: 1,4-Dioxane
dioxane.healthEffect: IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic). Contaminant in many sulfate-based cleansers.
dioxane.detail: Byproduct of manufacturing process for ethoxylated ingredients (SLES, PEG). Not listed on labels.

peg compounds.name: PEG Compounds
peg compounds.healthEffect: Can be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (possible carcinogen) and ethylene oxide during manufacturing.
peg compounds.detail: Found in creams, lotions, shampoos. The contamination risk depends on manufacturing quality.

caramel color.name: Caramel Color (E150d)
caramel color.healthEffect: Class IV caramel (E150d) contains 4-MEI, a possible carcinogen. California requires Prop 65 warning.
caramel color.detail: Found in colas, soy sauce, and dark beverages. The specific variant E150d is the concerning one.

potassium sorbate.name: Potassium Sorbate
potassium sorbate.healthEffect: Generally considered safe. One of the least concerning preservatives.
potassium sorbate.detail: Widely used and well-studied. No significant health concerns at food-grade levels.

maltodextrin.name: Maltodextrin
maltodextrin.healthEffect: Extremely high glycemic index (85-136). Spikes blood sugar faster than white sugar or glucose.
maltodextrin.detail: Hidden in many "health" products like Bournvita, Horlicks, Complan. Often used as cheap filler.
maltodextrin.summary: A highly processed starch-derived filler with a glycemic index HIGHER than pure sugar (GI up to 136 vs sugar GI 65). Often hidden in "health drinks" as a cheap bulking agent.

impacts.effect: Glycemic Index of 85-136 (higher than table sugar at 65). Rapid blood sugar spikes linked to insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes risk with chronic consumption.
May disrupt gut microbiome and promote growth of pathogenic bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella) in mouse studies
Associated with increased inflammatory bowel disease symptoms in susceptible individuals
impacts.sourceLabel: Laudisi et al., Cellular and Molecular Gastroenterology 2019

vulnerableGroups: Diabetics and pre-diabetics
Children (marketed "health drinks" target them)
People with IBD, Crohn's disease, or ulcerative colitis
Anyone managing weight or insulin resistance
foundInProducts: Sugar-free products (ironic — often higher GI than sugar)
maltodextrin.whatToDo: Check for "maltodextrin" or "modified starch" in the ingredient list. Be especially suspicious when it appears high on the ingredient list — that means it's a major component. Avoid in products marketed to children as "health drinks".
saferAlternatives: Natural sweeteners like honey or jaggery (moderate)

sources.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values
Maltodextrin impairs intestinal immune response and promotes gut pathology
The food additive maltodextrin promotes endoplasmic reticulum stress-driven mucus depletion
sources.authority: Laudisi et al., Cell Mol Gastroenterol Hepatol

high fructose corn syrup.name: High Fructose Corn Syrup
high fructose corn syrup.healthEffect: Linked to obesity, insulin resistance, and fatty liver disease with excess consumption.
high fructose corn syrup.detail: Metabolized differently than regular sugar. Associated with increased visceral fat.

artificial flavoring.name: Artificial Flavoring Substances
artificial flavoring.healthEffect: Undisclosed chemical composition. "Nature identical" is a regulatory loophole.
artificial flavoring.detail: "Nature identical flavouring substances" can contain dozens of undisclosed chemicals. FSSAI does not require individual disclosure.

interesterified fat.name: Interesterified Fat
interesterified fat.healthEffect: May raise blood glucose and lower HDL (good cholesterol). Less studied than trans fats.
interesterified fat.detail: Replaced trans fats in many products but may have its own health risks. Found in biscuits and bakery items.

acesulfame potassium.name: Acesulfame Potassium
acesulfame potassium.healthEffect: EFSA 2025 re-evaluation concluded no safety concern at current intakes; ADI raised from 9 to 15 mg/kg bw/day.
acesulfame potassium.detail: Artificial sweetener approved globally. EFSA 2025 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2025;23:9317) raised the ADI from 9 to 15 mg/kg bw/day and explicitly concluded intake is not associated with cancer, glucose/insulin disturbance, cardiovascular risk, or organ toxicity. FDA approved as general-purpose sweetener since 2003 (21 CFR 172.800). Rodent studies have shown altered gut microbiota composition, but human clinical significance is not established. The Suez 2022 Cell study on sweeteners and gut microbiome did not test acesulfame K.
acesulfame potassium.fdaStatus: Approved general-purpose sweetener since 2003 under 21 CFR 172.800

saccharin.name: Saccharin
saccharin.healthEffect: IARC Group 3 (1999, downgraded from 2B). Delisted from California Prop 65: saccharin April 6, 2001; sodium saccharin January 17, 2003.
saccharin.detail: Artificial sweetener. IARC downgraded saccharin from Group 2B to Group 3 in 1999 (IARC Monograph Vol. 73) after concluding that the rat bladder tumors observed in earlier studies were caused by a rodent-specific mechanism (urinary microcrystal formation) not relevant to humans. California Prop 65 delisted saccharin on April 6, 2001 and sodium saccharin on January 17, 2003. FDA permits saccharin as non-nutritive sweetener under 21 CFR 180.37; the US warning-label requirement was repealed by the Saccharin Notice Repeal Act of 2000. Suez et al. 2022 (Cell 185:3307) found that saccharin can alter glucose tolerance in some human subjects.
saccharin.fdaStatus: Approved non-nutritive sweetener under 21 CFR 180.37; warning label requirement repealed by Saccharin Notice Repeal Act 2000

sorbitol.name: Sorbitol
sorbitol.healthEffect: Can cause digestive upset in large amounts. Laxative effect.
sorbitol.detail: Sugar alcohol commonly found in sugar-free gum and candies.

xanthan gum.name: Xanthan Gum
xanthan gum.healthEffect: Generally safe. May cause bloating in sensitive people.
xanthan gum.detail: Used in gluten-free baking, sauces, salad dressings.

guar gum.name: Guar Gum
guar gum.healthEffect: Safe in small amounts. May cause digestive issues in large doses.
guar gum.detail: Natural fiber from guar beans. Common in Indian products.

sodium nitrite.name: Sodium Nitrite
sodium nitrite.healthEffect: Can form nitrosamines during cooking. IARC classifies ingested nitrites under conditions of endogenous nitrosation as Group 2A (probably carcinogenic).
sodium nitrite.detail: Used in cured meats (ham, bacon, sausages). WHO classifies processed meat as carcinogenic partly due to this.

sodium nitrate.name: Sodium Nitrate
sodium nitrate.healthEffect: Converts to sodium nitrite in the body. Same cancer concerns.
sodium nitrate.detail: Used in preserved meats and some cheeses.

sulfites.name: Sulfites
sulfites.healthEffect: EFSA withdrew the group ADI in 2022 citing an inadequate toxicity database. Triggers asthma attacks in approximately 1% of the general population and a higher proportion of asthmatics.
sulfites.detail: EFSA withdrew the group ADI in 2022 (EFSA Journal 2022;20:7594) due to inadequate toxicity database. Triggers asthma attacks in ~1% of population, higher in asthmatics.

monoglycerides.name: Mono- and Diglycerides
monoglycerides.healthEffect: EFSA 2017 concluded no safety concern at current exposure; safety could not be confirmed for infants under 16 weeks (EFSA 2021).
monoglycerides.detail: Emulsifier widely used in baked goods, margarine, and ice cream. EFSA 2017 (EFSA Journal 2017;15:5045) concluded no safety concern at current exposure. EFSA 2021 (EFSA Journal 2021;19:6885) could not confirm safety in infants under 16 weeks. May contain residual trans-fatty acids when manufactured from partially hydrogenated oils, though this has been reduced significantly in modern production following global trans-fat restrictions.

polysorbate 80.name: Polysorbate 80
polysorbate 80.healthEffect: Emerging evidence from rodent studies (Chassaing 2015, Nature) suggests possible gut-microbiome disruption and low-grade inflammation.
polysorbate 80.detail: Synthetic emulsifier. EFSA 2015 (EFSA Journal 2015;13:4152) set a group ADI of 25 mg/kg bw/day for polysorbates E432-E436. Chassaing et al. 2015 showed mice fed P80 at concentrations approximating human dietary exposure developed low-grade inflammation, metabolic syndrome and colitis. Human-trial evidence still limited.

phenoxyethanol.name: Phenoxyethanol
phenoxyethanol.healthEffect: EU restricts to 1% max. Skin irritant. Neurotoxic in concentrated form.
phenoxyethanol.detail: Common "paraben-free" alternative. Found in Mamaearth, WOW, and many "natural" brands.

sodium laureth sulfate.name: SLES (Sodium Laureth Sulfate)
sodium laureth sulfate.healthEffect: Can be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (possible carcinogen) during manufacturing.
sodium laureth sulfate.detail: Milder than SLS but contamination risk. Found in shampoos and body washes.

cocamide dea.name: Cocamide DEA
cocamide dea.healthEffect: IARC classified as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B). Banned in California.
cocamide dea.detail: Foaming agent in shampoos, body washes, liquid soaps.

bht cosmetic.name: BHT (Cosmetic)
bht cosmetic.detail: Used in lipsticks, moisturizers, and makeup as a preservative.

retinyl palmitate.name: Retinyl Palmitate
retinyl palmitate.healthEffect: May accelerate skin tumor growth when applied to skin exposed to sunlight.
retinyl palmitate.detail: Found in many sunscreens and anti-aging creams. EWG flags as concerning in sun-exposed products.

talc.name: Talc
talc.healthEffect: IARC reclassified talc (not containing asbestiform fibres) to Group 2A — probably carcinogenic to humans — in July 2024, upgraded from Group 3.
talc.detail: Used as anti-caking agent and tablet lubricant in supplements. IARC Monograph Volume 136 (July 2024) reclassified talc to Group 2A based on limited evidence for ovarian cancer in humans, sufficient evidence in experimental animals, and strong mechanistic evidence. Historical concerns about asbestos contamination apply primarily to non-food-grade talc.

dmdm hydantoin.name: DMDM Hydantoin
dmdm hydantoin.detail: Found in shampoos and conditioners. Class action lawsuits against Unilever products.

quaternium-15.name: Quaternium-15
quaternium-15.healthEffect: Formaldehyde releaser. Banned in EU cosmetics.
quaternium-15.detail: Still found in some Indian cosmetic products.

imidazolidinyl urea.name: Imidazolidinyl Urea
imidazolidinyl urea.detail: EU restricts to 0.6%. Found in shampoos and lotions.

ci 77891.name: CI 77891 (Titanium Dioxide)
ci 77891.healthEffect: Safe in cosmetics when not inhaled. Nanoparticle form controversial.
ci 77891.detail: Common white pigment in sunscreens, face powder, lipsticks.

mineral oil.name: Mineral Oil
mineral oil.healthEffect: Occlusive (blocks pores). Untreated forms are IARC Group 1 carcinogens.
mineral oil.detail: Cosmetic-grade is purified but still controversial. Found in baby oils, moisturizers.

ammonia.name: Ammonia
ammonia.healthEffect: Respiratory irritant. Mixing with bleach releases toxic chloramine gas.
ammonia.detail: Found in glass cleaners (Colin), floor cleaners, hair dyes.

sodium hypochlorite.name: Sodium Hypochlorite (Bleach)
sodium hypochlorite.healthEffect: Corrosive. Chlorine gas release when mixed with acids or ammonia.
sodium hypochlorite.detail: Active ingredient in Domex, some Harpic variants. Never mix with other cleaners.

hydrochloric acid.name: Hydrochloric Acid
hydrochloric acid.detail: Main ingredient in Harpic (9.5%). Handle with extreme care.

benzalkonium chloride.name: Benzalkonium Chloride
benzalkonium chloride.healthEffect: Skin irritant, asthma trigger. Banned in EU hand soaps (2017).
benzalkonium chloride.detail: Found in Lizol, disinfectant sprays, hand sanitizers.

cypermethrin.name: Cypermethrin
cypermethrin.healthEffect: EPA Group C possible carcinogen. Neurotoxic with chronic exposure.
cypermethrin.detail: Found in Hit aerosol spray and some mosquito repellents.

prallethrin.name: Prallethrin
prallethrin.healthEffect: Neurotoxic at high exposure. Children more sensitive.
prallethrin.detail: Active ingredient in Good Knight liquid vaporizer. AIIMS study found higher respiratory symptoms in children with daily use.

transfluthrin.name: Transfluthrin
transfluthrin.healthEffect: Similar concerns to other pyrethroids. Chronic exposure risks.
transfluthrin.detail: Used in All Out and other mosquito repellents.

allethrin.name: Allethrin
allethrin.healthEffect: Respiratory irritant. Combustion releases particulate matter.
allethrin.detail: Used in Mortein and other mosquito coils. Burning releases PM2.5.

diethyl phthalate incense.name: Diethyl Phthalate (in fragrances)
diethyl phthalate incense.healthEffect: Endocrine disruptor. Binding agent in agarbatti releases harmful compounds when burned.
diethyl phthalate incense.detail: Often hidden in "parfum" or "fragrance" ingredient listings. Widespread in Indian incense.

bha endocrine.name: Butyl Paraben
bha endocrine.healthEffect: Strongest estrogen-mimicking paraben. Banned in EU cosmetics for children under 3.
bha endocrine.detail: Found in cosmetics, often despite "paraben-free" marketing.

resorcinol.name: Resorcinol
resorcinol.detail: Common in hair dyes (Streax, Godrej), acne treatments.

ppd dye.name: PPD (p-Phenylenediamine)
ppd dye.healthEffect: Severe allergen. Multiple deaths in India from allergic reactions.
ppd dye.detail: Active ingredient in most hair dyes. EU limits to 2% and requires allergy testing.

lead acetate.name: Lead Acetate
lead acetate.healthEffect: Neurotoxin. Accumulates in body. Banned in EU cosmetics.
lead acetate.detail: Historically in hair dyes (Grecian Formula). Some Indian kohl/surma still contains lead.

mercury cosmetic.name: Mercury Compounds
mercury cosmetic.healthEffect: Neurotoxin. Kidney damage. Banned in EU and US cosmetics.
mercury cosmetic.detail: Illegally added to unregulated Indian fairness creams. Check skin-lightening products.

high fructose.name: Invert Sugar Syrup
high fructose.healthEffect: Rapidly absorbed. Spikes blood sugar faster than table sugar.
high fructose.detail: Common in Indian candies and sweetened beverages.

hydrogenated oil.name: Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil
hydrogenated oil.healthEffect: Trans fats. Linked to heart disease. Banned globally.
hydrogenated oil.detail: FDA banned in 2018. India limits to 2% by 2022. Still found in some cheap biscuits and snacks.

msg ext.name: Monopotassium Glutamate
msg ext.healthEffect: Similar to MSG. May trigger MSG symptom complex in sensitive individuals.
msg ext.detail: Potassium analog of MSG, used similarly.

calcium propionate.name: Calcium Propionate
calcium propionate.healthEffect: Linked to behavioral issues and sleep problems in some studies.
calcium propionate.detail: Common in packaged bread and baked goods.

potassium sorbate food.name: Potassium Metabisulfite
potassium sorbate food.healthEffect: Can trigger asthma and allergic reactions. Releases sulfur dioxide.
potassium sorbate food.detail: Found in wine, dried fruits, processed foods. EU mandates labeling.

acesulfame.name: Neotame
acesulfame.healthEffect: 13,000x sweeter than sugar. 2024 study suggested gut damage.
acesulfame.detail: Used in tiny amounts in baked goods, chewing gum, sports drinks.

palm oil.name: Palm Oil (Refined)
palm oil.healthEffect: High in saturated fat. Refined palm oil contains 3-MCPD and glycidyl esters, classified as probable carcinogens.
palm oil.detail: India is the world's largest importer of palm oil. Found in most Indian biscuits, noodles, and snacks.
palm oil.summary: A widely used vegetable oil. The refining process creates 3-MCPD and glycidyl esters - classified as probable carcinogens by IARC and EFSA. High in saturated fat.
palm oil.fdaStatus: Permitted; no specific limit on 3-MCPD in food

impacts.effect: Refined palm oil contains glycidyl esters (GE), which release glycidol - classified by IARC as "probably carcinogenic to humans" (Group 2A)
3-MCPD contaminants found in refined palm oil cause kidney damage and testicular tumors in animal studies
High in saturated fat (~50%). Excess consumption raises LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk
Animal studies show 3-MCPD reduces male fertility and disrupts testosterone production
impacts.sourceLabel: Cho et al., Toxicology and Applied Pharmacology, 2008

vulnerableGroups: Infants and young children (highest exposure per kg body weight from infant formula)
Heart disease patients or those with high cholesterol
Men (testicular/fertility concerns from 3-MCPD)
Heavy consumers of packaged biscuits, noodles, and fried snacks
palm oil.whatToDo: Check labels for "palm oil", "palmolein", "vegetable oil (palm)", or "palm kernel oil". Choose products that specify unrefined oils like cold-pressed coconut, sunflower, or mustard oil. EU has introduced maximum limits on 3-MCPD that India has not yet adopted.

sources.title: IARC Monograph Volume 77: Glycidol
Risks for human health related to the presence of 3- and 2-MCPD and glycidyl esters in food
EU Regulation 2020/1322 - Maximum levels of 3-MCPD in refined oils
WHO Dietary Fat Guidelines

metanil yellow.name: Metanil Yellow
metanil yellow.healthEffect: Non-permitted coal-tar dye. FSSAI explicitly prohibited. Linked to tumour development, neurotoxicity, and intestinal damage in animal studies.
metanil yellow.detail: Common illegal adulterant in turmeric (haldi), besan, dal, laddoo, and jalebi to enhance yellow colour. Routinely flagged in FSSAI raids on Indian spice markets.

rhodamine b.name: Rhodamine B
rhodamine b.healthEffect: Industrial dye banned for food use worldwide. Liver toxicity and carcinogenicity in animal studies. Genotoxic.
rhodamine b.detail: Used illegally in chili powder, paan masala, mithai (gulab jamun, jalebi), and lipsticks. FSSAI and US FDA prohibit food use. Commonly flagged in Indian mithai shop raids.

sudan dye.name: Sudan Dyes (I, II, III, IV)
sudan dye.healthEffect: IARC Group 3 but classified carcinogenic in animal studies. Genotoxic. Forms DNA adducts.
sudan dye.detail: Industrial dyes illegally added to chili powder, curry powder, and palm oil to intensify red colour. Multiple EU import bans on Indian chili shipments. FSSAI prohibited.

ethylene oxide.name: Ethylene Oxide
ethylene oxide.healthEffect: IARC Group 1 (carcinogenic to humans). Causes lymphoma and leukaemia. California Prop 65 listed for both cancer and reproductive toxicity.
ethylene oxide.detail: Used as a pesticide and sterilant on spice exports. In 2024, several Indian spice exports were recalled across Hong Kong, Singapore, Maldives, and Nepal for ethylene oxide contamination. Residues are carcinogenic even at low concentrations.

malachite green.name: Malachite Green
malachite green.healthEffect: Banned for food use globally. Carcinogenic, mutagenic, teratogenic. Persists in fish tissue long after application.
malachite green.detail: Illegally used to preserve freshness of fish in Indian wet markets. FSSAI and EU explicitly banned. Regularly flagged in India seafood export tests and coastal market raids.

melamine.name: Melamine
melamine.healthEffect: Kidney damage, kidney stones, death at high doses. 2008 China infant formula scandal killed 6 babies and sickened 300,000.
melamine.detail: Illegally added to milk and milk products to fake higher protein test readings. FSSAI tests Indian milk supply regularly. Also detected in imported pet food.

argemone oil.name: Argemone Oil
argemone oil.healthEffect: Causes epidemic dropsy. Liver damage, glaucoma, cardiac arrest, death. Caused mass poisonings in India (1998 Delhi, 2002 Madhya Pradesh).
argemone oil.detail: Cheap adulterant mixed into mustard oil (sarson ka tel). FSSAI explicitly prohibited. Outbreaks still periodically reported from rural India.

calcium carbide.name: Calcium Carbide
calcium carbide.healthEffect: Releases acetylene gas contaminated with arsenic and phosphorus. Causes stomach ulcers, skin rashes, and carcinogenic exposure.
calcium carbide.detail: Illegally used to ripen mangoes, bananas, papayas in Indian wholesale markets. FSSAI explicitly prohibited. Legal alternative is ethephon or natural ripening.

chlorpyrifos.name: Chlorpyrifos
chlorpyrifos.healthEffect: Organophosphate pesticide. Neurodevelopmental damage in children (reduced IQ, autism spectrum concerns). US EPA banned food use (2021). EU banned 2020.
chlorpyrifos.detail: Banned in 40+ countries but still used on Indian rice, wheat, and vegetables. FSSAI sets MRL limits but CSE tests routinely find residues exceeding them. Wash produce thoroughly.

glyphosate.name: Glyphosate
glyphosate.healthEffect: IARC Group 2A (probably carcinogenic to humans). Linked to non-Hodgkin lymphoma. California Prop 65 listed.
glyphosate.detail: Most-used herbicide globally. Residues detected in Indian wheat, oats, and pulses. Sri Lanka and 20+ countries banned it. India allows restricted use.

acrylamide.name: Acrylamide
acrylamide.healthEffect: IARC Group 2A (probably carcinogenic). Forms during high-heat cooking of starchy foods. California Prop 65 listed.
acrylamide.detail: Found in chips, fried namkeen, biscuits, fried potato items. EU set maximum benchmark levels in 2017. India has no limits. Reduce formation by avoiding deep browning.

3-mcpd.name: 3-MCPD
3-mcpd.healthEffect: Kidney damage and testicular tumours in animal studies. EFSA set strict ADI in 2018. EU regulated maximum levels in refined oils (2020).
3-mcpd.detail: Forms during high-temperature refining of vegetable oils, especially palm oil. Most Indian biscuits, chocolates, and noodles use refined palm oil. India has no regulatory limits.

glycidol.name: Glycidol
glycidol.healthEffect: IARC Group 2A (probably carcinogenic to humans). Genotoxic. California Prop 65 listed for cancer.
glycidol.detail: Formed alongside 3-MCPD during refining of vegetable oils. Palm oil is the worst offender. Found in most Indian processed foods that use refined oils.

brominated vegetable oil.name: Brominated Vegetable Oil (BVO)
brominated vegetable oil.healthEffect: Bromine accumulates in fatty tissue. Linked to neurological symptoms, skin lesions, and memory loss at high exposure.
brominated vegetable oil.detail: Banned in UK (1970), India (1990), EU (2008), Japan (2010), US (2024), and Canada (2024). India prohibited BVO in soft drinks in 1990. Check labels for any ingredient listed as "brominated".
brominated vegetable oil.fdaStatus: Banned by FDA effective August 2, 2024 (compliance required by August 2, 2025); final rule 89 FR 55040.

vanaspati.name: Vanaspati (Partially Hydrogenated Oil)
vanaspati.healthEffect: Raises LDL cholesterol and lowers HDL. Causes heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes. WHO calls for global elimination.
vanaspati.detail: Vanaspati/Dalda is widely used in Indian mithai, samosas, pakoras, and bakery items. FSSAI set a 2% trans fat limit in 2022 but enforcement is weak. WHO identifies India as a high-burden country.

red lead cosmetic.name: Red Lead (Sindoor Adulterant)
red lead cosmetic.healthEffect: Lead poisoning. Neurotoxic in adults and especially children. IARC Group 2A. Causes permanent brain damage and developmental delays.
red lead cosmetic.detail: Cheap adulterant in traditional Indian sindoor (vermillion), kumkum, and kohl. FSSAI and BIS limit lead in cosmetics but compliance is weak in unbranded products. Choose branded ISI-marked products.

4-mei.name: 4-Methylimidazole (4-MEI)
4-mei.healthEffect: Forms during manufacture of Class III and Class IV caramel colour (E150c, E150d). California Prop 65 listed for cancer at levels above 29 micrograms per day.
4-mei.detail: Found in dark colas, soy sauce, dark beers, and caramel-coloured sauces. Major cola brands reformulated in California (2012) to avoid Prop 65 warning labels. India has no regulatory limits.

formalin.name: Formalin (Fish Preservative)
formalin.healthEffect: IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Eye and throat irritation, nausea, long-term cancer risk. Residues remain in fish tissue after washing.
formalin.detail: Illegally sprayed on fish in Indian coastal markets to preserve appearance. Kerala, Goa, and Andhra Pradesh have repeatedly flagged adulterated fish in raids. FSSAI explicitly prohibited.

propyl gallate.name: Propyl Gallate
propyl gallate.healthEffect: EFSA lowered ADI to 0.5 mg/kg bw/day in 2014 due to reproductive-toxicity data limitations.
propyl gallate.detail: Synthetic antioxidant used to prevent rancidity in fats and oils. EFSA ANS Panel 2014 (EFSA Journal 2014;12(4):3642) lowered the ADI from 1.4 to 0.5 mg/kg bw/day, applying a 300x uncertainty factor due to subchronic-to-chronic extrapolation and limitations in the reproductive-toxicity database.
propyl gallate.fdaStatus: GRAS for use as antioxidant in food (21 CFR 184.1660)

sodium cyclamate.name: Sodium Cyclamate
sodium cyclamate.healthEffect: Banned in the US since 1970; currently classified IARC Group 3 (not classifiable as carcinogen to humans).
sodium cyclamate.detail: FDA banned cyclamate in food in 1970 (21 CFR 189.135) based on a 1969 rat study suggesting bladder cancer when combined with saccharin. IARC Group 3 as of 1999 — subsequent studies have not confirmed carcinogenicity. Permitted in EU, Canada, India, and 130+ other countries.
sodium cyclamate.fdaStatus: Banned for direct addition to food since 1970 (21 CFR 189.135); ban not rescinded despite 1989 FDA reassessment

benzoic acid.name: Benzoic Acid
benzoic acid.healthEffect: Group ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day with benzoates. High intake in children can approach ADI.
benzoic acid.detail: Antimicrobial preservative. EFSA 2016 (EFSA Journal 2016;14(3):4433) set the group ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day for benzoic acid and its sodium, potassium, and calcium salts. Can cause pseudo-allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

potassium benzoate.name: Potassium Benzoate
potassium benzoate.healthEffect: Shares group ADI with benzoic acid; same pseudo-allergic concerns in sensitive individuals.
potassium benzoate.detail: Antimicrobial preservative covered by the EFSA 2016 group ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day with E210, E211, and E213.

calcium benzoate.name: Calcium Benzoate
calcium benzoate.healthEffect: Group ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day with benzoic acid and other benzoates.
calcium benzoate.detail: Antimicrobial preservative. Covered by EFSA 2016 benzoate group ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day.

potassium nitrite.name: Potassium Nitrite
potassium nitrite.healthEffect: IARC classifies ingested nitrites under endogenous-nitrosation conditions as Group 2A (probably carcinogenic).
potassium nitrite.detail: Used as a curing agent in meat. IARC Monograph Vol. 94 (2010) Group 2A. EFSA 2017 group ADI is 0.07 mg nitrite ion/kg bw/day (EFSA Journal 2017;15:4786). Exposure from food-additive use can exceed the ADI in some sub-groups.
potassium nitrite.fdaStatus: Permitted curing agent in specified meat products (21 CFR 172.160)

potassium nitrate.name: Potassium Nitrate
potassium nitrate.healthEffect: IARC Group 2A for ingested nitrate under endogenous-nitrosation conditions (nitrate reduces to nitrite in vivo).
potassium nitrate.detail: Used as a preservative and curing agent. IARC Monograph Vol. 94 (2010) — Group 2A under endogenous-nitrosation conditions. EFSA 2017 group ADI 3.7 mg nitrate ion/kg bw/day (EFSA Journal 2017;15:4787).
potassium nitrate.fdaStatus: Permitted curing agent in specified cured meat/fish (21 CFR 172.160)

polysorbate 60.name: Polysorbate 60
polysorbate 60.healthEffect: Same class as polysorbate 80; possible gut-microbiome effects under investigation.
polysorbate 60.detail: Synthetic emulsifier, part of EFSA 2015 group ADI of 25 mg/kg bw/day for polysorbates.

sodium cmc.name: Sodium Carboxymethyl Cellulose
sodium cmc.healthEffect: Human randomised-controlled-feeding trial (Chassaing 2022) showed CMC altered gut microbiome and caused microbial encroachment into mucus layer.
sodium cmc.detail: Thickener/emulsifier. Chassaing et al. 2022 (Gastroenterology 162:743-756) — RCT with 15 g/day CMC increased abdominal discomfort, altered microbiome composition, and depleted short-chain fatty acids. EFSA 2022 (EFSA Journal 2022;20:7665) could not confirm ADI for infants under 16 weeks.

silicon dioxide.name: Silicon Dioxide
silicon dioxide.healthEffect: EFSA 2018 could not confirm previous ADI due to concerns about nano-scale particles; EFSA 2024 applied margin-of-exposure approach.
silicon dioxide.detail: Anti-caking agent used in powdered foods and supplements. EFSA 2018 (EFSA Journal 2018;16:5088) flagged nanoparticle-content concerns. EFSA 2024 (EFSA Journal 2024;22:8880) applied a margin-of-exposure approach and concluded no safety concern at current levels but did not set a numerical ADI.

quinoline yellow.name: Quinoline Yellow
quinoline yellow.healthEffect: Not permitted for food use in the US. EU Southampton Six — mandatory hyperactivity warning label required.
quinoline yellow.detail: Synthetic yellow dye. EFSA 2009 lowered ADI from 10 to 0.5 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA Journal 2009;7:1329). Not authorised as food colour in the US (permitted only as D&C Yellow No. 10 in drugs/cosmetics). EU Regulation 1333/2008 Annex V requires the warning 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' on products containing it (Southampton Study, McCann et al., Lancet 2007).

amaranth color.name: Amaranth (FD&C Red No. 2)
amaranth color.healthEffect: Banned for food use in the US since February 1976.
amaranth color.detail: Synthetic red dye. FDA banned amaranth for food use in 1976 following rat carcinogenicity concerns (Federal Register, Feb 10 and 13, 1976). EFSA 2010 (EFSA Journal 2010;8:1649) lowered the ADI from 0.8 to 0.15 mg/kg bw/day. Still permitted in India and EU under specified limits.

green s.name: Green S
green s.healthEffect: Not authorised for food use in the US; EFSA ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day.
green s.detail: Synthetic green dye. Not permitted in US food. EFSA 2010 (EFSA Journal 2010;8:1851) ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day.

fast green fcf.name: Fast Green FCF
fast green fcf.healthEffect: Not authorised as a food additive in the EU (Regulation 1333/2008 Annex II).
fast green fcf.detail: Synthetic green dye. Permitted in US (21 CFR 74.203) and permitted as one of the 8 synthetic colours in India (FSS Regulations 2011). Not authorised in the EU.

brilliant black bn.name: Brilliant Black BN
brilliant black bn.healthEffect: Not authorised in the US. EFSA flagged that children's 95th-percentile exposure can approach the ADI.
brilliant black bn.detail: Synthetic black dye. Not permitted in US food. EFSA 2010 (EFSA Journal 2010;8:1540) ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day; JECFA ADI 1 mg/kg bw/day. Exposure-refinement opinion in 2015 noted 95th-percentile children intake can reach ADI range.

brown ht.name: Brown HT
brown ht.healthEffect: Not authorised in the US. EU Southampton Six — mandatory hyperactivity warning.
brown ht.detail: Synthetic brown dye. Not permitted in US food. EFSA 2010 ADI 1.5 mg/kg bw/day. Southampton Six — EU Regulation 1333/2008 Annex V warning label required (McCann 2007 Lancet Southampton Study).

patent blue v.name: Patent Blue V
patent blue v.healthEffect: Not authorised in the US. Documented hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis risk (mainly from medical-dye use).
patent blue v.detail: Synthetic blue dye. Not permitted in US food. EFSA 2013 (EFSA Journal 2013;11:2818) ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day. Documented anaphylaxis reactions when used as medical tracer dye.

indigo carmine.name: Indigo Carmine
indigo carmine.healthEffect: Permitted globally; EFSA 2014 raised no genotoxicity concern.
indigo carmine.detail: Synthetic blue dye. EFSA 2014 (EFSA Journal 2014;12:3768) set ADI 5 mg/kg bw/day; follow-up 2023 confirmed. Permitted in US (21 CFR 74.102) and as one of 8 FSSAI-permitted synthetic colours.

monopotassium glutamate.name: Monopotassium Glutamate
monopotassium glutamate.healthEffect: Shares MSG-class sensitivity pattern; covered by glutamates group ADI of 30 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA 2017).
monopotassium glutamate.detail: Potassium salt of glutamic acid. EFSA 2017 (EFSA Journal 2017;15:4910) set a group ADI of 30 mg/kg bw/day for E620-E625 glutamates.

disodium 5 ribonucleotides.name: Disodium 5'-Ribonucleotides
disodium 5 ribonucleotides.healthEffect: 1:1 mixture of disodium guanylate and inosinate; shares purine content/gout concerns.
disodium 5 ribonucleotides.detail: Commercial mixture of E627 and E631 (typically 1:1). JECFA ADI 'not specified'.

datem.name: DATEM (Diacetyltartaric Esters of Mono/Diglycerides)
aliases: diacetyltartaric acid esters of mono and diglycerides
datem.healthEffect: EFSA 2017 maintained ADI 'not specified' but noted data gaps; preliminary emulsifier-class gut concerns.
datem.detail: Dough strengthener/emulsifier common in bread. EFSA 2017 (EFSA Journal 2017;15:4912) reevaluation noted database limitations. Mouse studies at very high doses showed cardiac effects (not considered relevant at typical exposure).
